summary: DHH explains how Basecamp manages programming projects without tightly linking todos to code commits. He argues that the pull request already provides sufficient connection between planning and implementation, making micro-linking unnecessary overhead. Using the Out of Office feature as a case study, he shows how they split work between Basecamp (scope, decisions, trade-offs) and GitHub (code, reviews, CI). He contrasts this approach with Jira-style ticket factories, positioning Basecamp's method as part of their broader Shape Up philosophy.
key_points: You don't need to tie every todo to every commit — pull requests already provide the right level of granularity for connecting planning to code
Most features at Basecamp have a 1-1 relationship between a Basecamp project and a pull request, keeping things simple
Project management is split cleanly: Basecamp handles scope, decisions, and trade-offs while GitHub handles code, reviews, and CI
Managing projects is mostly about communication and human context, not procedural linking of artifacts
The Jira ticket-pulling assembly-line mindset is fundamentally incompatible with how Basecamp thinks about work
money_quotes: Micromanaging that level of detail has the appeal of superficial neatness, but without much if any payoff in additional ease or insight in practical terms.

The entire story is told in two parts: The Basecamp project that sets the scope, manages the outstanding work, documents the trade-offs and the considerations. The GitHub repository that holds the code, the per-line review notes, and the results of continuous integration testing.
Managing projects, be they code or otherwise, is mostly about communication.

Making decisions about how things should work, doing the trade-offs to ship within the time allotted. That's the kind of work that benefits from narratives, selective highlighting, and human context far more than it does from some procedural idea of Tying It All Together.
Programmers are trained in consistency and coherence. They're key properties of writing good code. But sometimes chasing those properties outside the code just don't offset the cost of doing so.
I get that this might feel somewhat alien if you're working at a feature factory pulling tickets from a Jira backlog.
key_insight: Project management doesn't need granular commit-to-ticket traceability — a single pull request per feature and clear narrative communication provide all the context teams actually need.
